title: 284. Peeking Iterator
question:
Given an Iterator class interface with methods: next() and hasNext(), design and implement a PeekingIterator that support the peek() operation -- it essentially peek() at the element that will be returned by the next call to next().

Example:

Assume that the iterator is initialized to the beginning of the list: [1,2,3].

Call next() gets you 1, the first element in the list.
Now you call peek() and it returns 2, the next element. Calling next() after that still return 2. 
You call next() the final time and it returns 3, the last element. 
Calling hasNext() after that should return false.

Follow up: How would you extend your design to be generic and work with all types, not just integer?
code: # Below is the interface for Iterator, which is already defined for you.\u000A#\u000A# class Iterator(object):\u000A#     def __init__(self, nums):\u000A#         \u0022\u0022\u0022\u000A#         Initializes an iterator object to the beginning of a list.\u000A#         :type nums: List[int]\u000A#         \u0022\u0022\u0022\u000A#\u000A#     def hasNext(self):\u000A#         \u0022\u0022\u0022\u000A#         Returns true if the iteration has more elements.\u000A#         :rtype: bool\u000A#         \u0022\u0022\u0022\u000A#\u000A#     def next(self):\u000A#         \u0022\u0022\u0022\u000A#         Returns the next element in the iteration.\u000A#         :rtype: int\u000A#         \u0022\u0022\u0022\u000A\u000Aclass PeekingIterator(object):\u000A    def __init__(self, iterator):\u000A        \u0022\u0022\u0022\u000A        Initialize your data structure here.\u000A        :type iterator: Iterator\u000A        \u0022\u0022\u0022\u000A        self.iterator \u003D iterator\u000A        self.cache \u003D None\u000A\u000A    def peek(self):\u000A        \u0022\u0022\u0022\u000A        Returns the next element in the iteration without advancing the iterator.\u000A        :rtype: int\u000A        \u0022\u0022\u0022\u000A        if self.cache is None and self.iterator.hasNext():\u000A            self.cache \u003D self.iterator.next()\u000A        return self.cache\u000A\u000A    def next(self):\u000A        \u0022\u0022\u0022\u000A        :rtype: int\u000A        \u0022\u0022\u0022\u000A        self.peek()\u000A        cache \u003D self.cache\u000A        self.cache \u003D None\u000A        return cache\u000A\u000A    def hasNext(self):\u000A        \u0022\u0022\u0022\u000A        :rtype: bool\u000A        \u0022\u0022\u0022\u000A        self.peek()\u000A        return not bool(self.cache is None)\u000A\u000A# Your PeekingIterator object will be instantiated and called as such:\u000A# iter \u003D PeekingIterator(Iterator(nums))\u000A# while iter.hasNext():\u000A#     val \u003D iter.peek()   # Get the next element but not advance the iterator.\u000A#     iter.next()         # Should return the same value as [val].
